question: $\sqrt[3]{n} - \sqrt[3]{m}$ ($n,m = 0, 1, 2, \dots$)?
solution:
Solution 1. By the binomial expansion,
\[
\sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n}=n^{1 / 3}\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^{1 / 3}-n^{1 / 3}=n^{1 / 3}\left(1+O\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)\right)-n^{1 / 3}=O\left(n^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
so \( \sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \). (Alternatively, one could use
\[
\sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n}=\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{(n+1)^{2}}+\sqrt[3]{n(n+1)}+\sqrt[3]{n^{2}}}=O\left(n^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or
\[
\sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n}=\frac{1}{3} \int_{n}^{n+1} x^{-2 / 3} d x=O\left(n^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or the Mean Value Theorem applied to the difference quotient \( \frac{\sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n}}{(n+1)-n} \).)
If \( m>r^{3} \), then the numbers
\[
0=\sqrt[3]{m}-\sqrt[3]{m}<\sqrt[3]{m+1}-\sqrt[3]{m}<\cdots<\sqrt[3]{m+7 m}-\sqrt[3]{m}=\sqrt[3]{m}
\]
partition the interval \( [0, \sqrt[3]{m}] \), containing \( r \), in such a way that the largest subinterval is of size \( O\left(m^{-2 / 3}\right) \). By taking \( m \) sufficiently large, one can find a difference among these that is arbitrarily close to \( r \).

Remark. We show more generally, that for any sequence \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \) with \( a_{n} \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( a_{n+1}-a_{n} \rightarrow 0 \), the set \( S=\left\{a_{n}-a_{m}: m, n \geq 0\right\} \) is dense in \( \mathbb{R} \). Given \( r \geq 0 \) and \( \epsilon>0 \), fix \( m \) such that \( \left|a_{M+1}-a_{M}\right|<\epsilon \) for all \( M \geq m \). If \( n \) is the smallest integer \( \geq m \) with \( a_{n} \geq a_{m}+r \), then \( a_{n}<a_{m}+r+\epsilon \), so \( a_{n}-a_{m} \) is within \( \epsilon \) of \( r \). This shows that \( S \) is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), and by symmetry \( S \) is dense also in \( (-\infty, 0] \).

Remark. Let \( f(x) \) be a function such that \( f(x) \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( f^{\prime}(x) \rightarrow 0 \) as \( x \rightarrow+\infty \). The Mean Value Theorem shows that the hypotheses of the previous remark are satisfied by the sequence \( a_{n}=f(n) \).

Solution 2. Fix \( r \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( \epsilon>0 \). Then for sufficiently large positive integers \( n \),
\[
(n+r)^{3}-(n+r-\epsilon)^{3}=3 n^{2} \epsilon+O(n)>1
\]
so \( (n+r-\epsilon)^{3} \leq\left\lfloor(n+r)^{3}\right\rfloor \leq(n+r)^{3} \), and \( \sqrt[3]{\left\lfloor(n+r)^{3}\right\rfloor} \) is within \( \epsilon \) of \( n+r \). Hence
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\sqrt[3]{\left\lfloor(n+r)^{3}\right\rfloor}-\sqrt[3]{n}\right)=r
\]

Solution 3. As in Solution \( 1, \sqrt[3]{n+1}-\sqrt[3]{n} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \), so the set \( S=\{\sqrt[3]{n}-\sqrt[3]{m}\} \) contains arbitrarily small positive numbers. Also \( S \) is closed under multiplication by positive integers \( k \) since \( k(\sqrt[3]{n}-\sqrt[3]{m})=\sqrt[3]{k^{3} n}-\sqrt[3]{k^{3} m} \). Any set with the preceding two properties is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), because any finite open interval \( (a, b) \) in \( [0, \infty) \) contains a multiple of any element of \( S \cap(0, b-a) \). By symmetry \( S \) is dense in \( (-\infty, 0] \) too.

Solution 4. Let \( b_{n}=(n \sqrt[3]{5} \bmod 1) \in[0,1] \). As in the remark following 1988B3, \( \left\{b_{n}\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \). Thus given \( \epsilon>0 \), we can find \( n \) such that \( n \sqrt[3]{5}-r \) is within \( \epsilon \) of some integer \( m \geq 0 \). Then \( \sqrt[3]{5 n^{3}}-\sqrt[3]{m^{3}}=n \sqrt[3]{5}-m \) is within \( \epsilon \) of \( r \).

descriptive_long.question: $\sqrt[3]{indexer} - \sqrt[3]{counter}$ ($indexer, counter = 0, 1, 2, \dots$)?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. By the binomial expansion,
\[
\sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}=indexer^{1 / 3}\left(1+\frac{1}{indexer}\right)^{1 / 3}-indexer^{1 / 3}=indexer^{1 / 3}\left(1+O\left(\frac{1}{indexer}\right)\right)-indexer^{1 / 3}=O\left(indexer^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
so \( \sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( indexer \rightarrow \infty \). (Alternatively, one could use
\[
\sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}=\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{(indexer+1)^{2}}+\sqrt[3]{indexer(indexer+1)}+\sqrt[3]{indexer^{2}}}=O\left(indexer^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or
\[
\sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}=\frac{1}{3} \int_{indexer}^{indexer+1} coordinate^{-2 / 3}\,d coordinate=O\left(indexer^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or the Mean Value Theorem applied to the difference quotient \( \frac{\sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}}{(indexer+1)-indexer} \).)
If \( counter>desired^{3} \), then the numbers
\[
0=\sqrt[3]{counter}-\sqrt[3]{counter}<\sqrt[3]{counter+1}-\sqrt[3]{counter}<\cdots<\sqrt[3]{counter+7\,counter}-\sqrt[3]{counter}=\sqrt[3]{counter}
\]
partition the interval \( [0, \sqrt[3]{counter}] \), containing \( desired \), in such a way that the largest subinterval is of size \( O\left(counter^{-2 / 3}\right) \). By taking \( counter \) sufficiently large, one can find a difference among these that is arbitrarily close to \( desired \).

Remark. We show more generally that for any sequence \( \{seqtermn\} \) with \( seqtermn \rightarrow +\infty \) and \( a_{indexer+1}-seqtermn \rightarrow 0 \), the set \( denseset=\{seqtermn-seqtermm:\,counter, indexer \ge 0\} \) is dense in \( \mathbb{R} \). Given \( desired \ge 0 \) and \( tolerance>0 \), fix \( counter \) such that \( |a_{bigindex+1}-seqtermbig|<tolerance \) for all \( bigindex \ge counter \). If \( indexer \) is the smallest integer \( \ge counter \) with \( a_{indexer} \ge seqtermm+desired \), then \( a_{indexer}<seqtermm+desired+tolerance \), so \( a_{indexer}-seqtermm \) is within \( tolerance \) of \( desired \). This shows that \( denseset \) is dense in \( [0,\infty) \); by symmetry it is dense also in \( (-\infty,0] \).

Remark. Let \( funcmain(coordinate) \) be a function such that \( funcmain(coordinate) \rightarrow +\infty \) and \( funcmain^{\prime}(coordinate) \rightarrow 0 \) as \( coordinate \rightarrow +\infty \). The Mean Value Theorem shows that the hypotheses of the previous remark are satisfied by the sequence \( seqtermn=funcmain(indexer) \).

Solution 2. Fix \( desired \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( tolerance>0 \). For sufficiently large positive integers \( indexer \),
\[
(indexer+desired)^{3}-(indexer+desired-tolerance)^{3}=3\,indexer^{2}\,tolerance+O(indexer)>1,
\]
so \( (indexer+desired-tolerance)^{3}\le \lfloor(indexer+desired)^{3}\rfloor \le (indexer+desired)^{3} \), and \( \sqrt[3]{\lfloor(indexer+desired)^{3}\rfloor} \) is within \( tolerance \) of \( indexer+desired \). Hence
\[
\lim_{indexer\to\infty}\bigl(\sqrt[3]{\lfloor(indexer+desired)^{3}\rfloor}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}\bigr)=desired.
\]

Solution 3. As in Solution 1, \( \sqrt[3]{indexer+1}-\sqrt[3]{indexer}\to0 \) as \( indexer\to\infty \), so the set \( denseset=\{\sqrt[3]{indexer}-\sqrt[3]{counter}\} \) contains arbitrarily small positive numbers. Also \( denseset \) is closed under multiplication by positive integers \( multfact \), since
\[
multfact\bigl(\sqrt[3]{indexer}-\sqrt[3]{counter}\bigr)=\sqrt[3]{multfact^{3}indexer}-\sqrt[3]{multfact^{3}counter}.
\]
Any set with these two properties is dense in \( [0,\infty) \), because any finite open interval \( (a,b) \subset [0,\infty) \) contains a multiple of an element of \( denseset\cap(0,b-a) \). By symmetry, \( denseset \) is dense in \( (-\infty,0] \) as well.

Solution 4. Let \( seqbterm=(indexer\sqrt[3]{5}\bmod1)\in[0,1] \). As in the remark following 1988B3, \( \{seqbterm\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \). Thus, given \( tolerance>0 \), we can find \( indexer \) such that \( indexer\sqrt[3]{5}-desired \) is within \( tolerance \) of some integer \( counter\ge0 \). Then
\[
\sqrt[3]{5\,indexer^{3}}-\sqrt[3]{counter^{3}}=indexer\sqrt[3]{5}-counter
\]
is within \( tolerance \) of \( desired \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: $\sqrt[3]{marigolds} - \sqrt[3]{skylarkic}$ ($marigolds,skylarkic = 0, 1, 2, \dots$)?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. By the binomial expansion,
\[
\sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds}=marigolds^{1 / 3}\left(1+\frac{1}{marigolds}\right)^{1 / 3}-marigolds^{1 / 3}=marigolds^{1 / 3}\left(1+O\left(\frac{1}{marigolds}\right)\right)-marigolds^{1 / 3}=O\left(marigolds^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
so \( \sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( marigolds \rightarrow \infty \). (Alternatively, one could use
\[
\sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds}=\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{(marigolds+1)^{2}}+\sqrt[3]{marigolds(marigolds+1)}+\sqrt[3]{marigolds^{2}}}=O\left(marigolds^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or
\[
\sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds}=\frac{1}{3} \int_{marigolds}^{marigolds+1} tangerine^{-2 / 3} d tangerine=O\left(marigolds^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or the Mean Value Theorem applied to the difference quotient \( \frac{\sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds}}{(marigolds+1)-marigolds} \).)
If \( skylarkic>rainshadow^{3} \), then the numbers
\[
0=\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}<\sqrt[3]{skylarkic+1}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}<\cdots<\sqrt[3]{skylarkic+7 skylarkic}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}=\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}
\]
partition the interval \( [0, \sqrt[3]{skylarkic}] \), containing \( rainshadow \), in such a way that the largest subinterval is of size \( O\left(skylarkic^{-2 / 3}\right) \). By taking \( skylarkic \) sufficiently large, one can find a difference among these that is arbitrarily close to \( rainshadow \).

Remark. We show more generally, that for any sequence \( \{buttercups\} \) with \( buttercups \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( a_{marigolds+1}-buttercups \rightarrow 0 \), the set \( pebblerock=\{buttercups-caterpillar: skylarkic, marigolds \geq 0\} \) is dense in \( \mathbb{R} \). Given \( rainshadow \geq 0 \) and \( lavendereps>0 \), fix \( skylarkic \) such that \( |a_{windflower+1}-dandelions|<lavendereps \) for all \( windflower \geq skylarkic \). If \( marigolds \) is the smallest integer \( \geq skylarkic \) with \( a_{marigolds} \geq a_{skylarkic}+rainshadow \), then \( a_{marigolds}<a_{skylarkic}+rainshadow+lavendereps \), so \( a_{marigolds}-a_{skylarkic} \) is within \( lavendereps \) of \( rainshadow \). This shows that \( pebblerock \) is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), and by symmetry \( pebblerock \) is dense also in \( (-\infty, 0] \).

Remark. Let \( watercress(tangerine) \) be a function such that \( watercress(tangerine) \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( watercress^{\prime}(tangerine) \rightarrow 0 \) as \( tangerine \rightarrow+\infty \). The Mean Value Theorem shows that the hypotheses of the previous remark are satisfied by the sequence \( buttercups=watercress(marigolds) \).

Solution 2. Fix \( rainshadow \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( lavendereps>0 \). Then for sufficiently large positive integers \( marigolds \),
\[
(marigolds+rainshadow)^{3}-(marigolds+rainshadow-lavendereps)^{3}=3 marigolds^{2} lavendereps+O(marigolds)>1
\]
so \( (marigolds+rainshadow-lavendereps)^{3} \leq\lfloor(marigolds+rainshadow)^{3}\rfloor \leq(marigolds+rainshadow)^{3} \), and \( \sqrt[3]{\lfloor(marigolds+rainshadow)^{3}\rfloor} \) is within \( lavendereps \) of \( marigolds+rainshadow \). Hence
\[
\lim_{marigolds \rightarrow \infty}\left(\sqrt[3]{\lfloor(marigolds+rainshadow)^{3}\rfloor}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds}\right)=rainshadow
\]

Solution 3. As in Solution 1, \( \sqrt[3]{marigolds+1}-\sqrt[3]{marigolds} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( marigolds \rightarrow \infty \), so the set \( pebblerock=\{\sqrt[3]{marigolds}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic}\} \) contains arbitrarily small positive numbers. Also \( pebblerock \) is closed under multiplication by positive integers \( porcupine \) since \( porcupine(\sqrt[3]{marigolds}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic})=\sqrt[3]{porcupine^{3} marigolds}-\sqrt[3]{porcupine^{3} skylarkic} \). Any set with the preceding two properties is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), because any finite open interval \( (a, b) \) in \( [0, \infty) \) contains a multiple of any element of \( pebblerock \cap(0, b-a) \). By symmetry \( pebblerock \) is dense in \( (-\infty, 0] \) too.

Solution 4. Let \( hummingbird=(marigolds \sqrt[3]{5} \bmod 1) \in[0,1] \). As in the remark following 1988B3, \( \{hummingbird\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \). Thus given \( lavendereps>0 \), we can find \( marigolds \) such that \( marigolds \sqrt[3]{5}-rainshadow \) is within \( lavendereps \) of some integer \( skylarkic \geq 0 \). Then \( \sqrt[3]{5 marigolds^{3}}-\sqrt[3]{skylarkic^{3}}=marigolds \sqrt[3]{5}-skylarkic \) is within \( lavendereps \) of \( rainshadow \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: $\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant} - \sqrt[3]{staticvalue}$ ($fixedconstant,staticvalue = 0, 1, 2, \dots$)?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. By the binomial expansion,
\[
\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}=fixedconstant^{1 / 3}\left(1+\frac{1}{fixedconstant}\right)^{1 / 3}-fixedconstant^{1 / 3}=fixedconstant^{1 / 3}\left(1+O\left(\frac{1}{fixedconstant}\right)\right)-fixedconstant^{1 / 3}=O\left(fixedconstant^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
so \( \sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( fixedconstant \rightarrow \infty \). (Alternatively, one could use
\[
\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}=\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{(fixedconstant+1)^{2}}+\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant(fixedconstant+1)}+\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant^{2}}}=O\left(fixedconstant^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or
\[
\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}=\frac{1}{3} \int_{fixedconstant}^{fixedconstant+1} stationarypoint^{-2 / 3} \, d\, stationarypoint=O\left(fixedconstant^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or the Mean Value Theorem applied to the difference quotient \( \frac{\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}}{(fixedconstant+1)-fixedconstant} \).)
If \( staticvalue>fixedreference^{3} \), then the numbers
\[
0=\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}<\sqrt[3]{staticvalue+1}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}<\cdots<\sqrt[3]{staticvalue+7 staticvalue}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}=\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}
\]
partition the interval \( [0, \sqrt[3]{staticvalue}] \), containing \( fixedreference \), in such a way that the largest subinterval is of size \( O\left(staticvalue^{-2 / 3}\right) \). By taking \( staticvalue \) sufficiently large, one can find a difference among these that is arbitrarily close to \( fixedreference \).

Remark. We show more generally that for any sequence \( \{constantsequence\} \) with \( constantsequence \rightarrow +\infty \) and \( constantsequenceplusone-constantsequence \rightarrow 0 \), the set \( sparsecollection=\{constantsequence-steadysequence: staticvalue,fixedconstant \ge 0\} \) is dense in \( \mathbb{R} \). Given \( fixedreference \ge 0 \) and \( hugeerror>0 \), fix \( staticvalue \) such that \( |stablesequenceplusone-stablesequence|<hugeerror \) for all \( settledindex \ge staticvalue \). If \( fixedconstant \) is the smallest integer \( \ge staticvalue \) with \( constantsequence \ge steadysequence+fixedreference \), then \( constantsequence<steadysequence+fixedreference+hugeerror \), so \( constantsequence-steadysequence \) is within \( hugeerror \) of \( fixedreference \). This shows that \( sparsecollection \) is dense in \( [0,\infty) \), and by symmetry \( sparsecollection \) is dense also in \( (-\infty,0] \).

Remark. Let \( stagnantmap(stationarypoint) \) be a function such that \( stagnantmap(stationarypoint) \rightarrow +\infty \) and \( stagnantmap' (stationarypoint) \rightarrow 0 \) as \( stationarypoint \rightarrow +\infty \). The Mean Value Theorem shows that the hypotheses of the previous remark are satisfied by the sequence \( constantsequence=stagnantmap(fixedconstant) \).

Solution 2. Fix \( fixedreference \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( hugeerror>0 \). Then for sufficiently large positive integers \( fixedconstant \),
\[
(fixedconstant+fixedreference)^{3}-(fixedconstant+fixedreference-hugeerror)^{3}=3 fixedconstant^{2} hugeerror+O(fixedconstant)>1
\]
so \( (fixedconstant+fixedreference-hugeerror)^{3} \le \lfloor(fixedconstant+fixedreference)^{3}\rfloor \le (fixedconstant+fixedreference)^{3} \), and \( \sqrt[3]{\lfloor(fixedconstant+fixedreference)^{3}\rfloor} \) is within \( hugeerror \) of \( fixedconstant+fixedreference \). Hence
\[
\lim_{fixedconstant \to \infty}\Bigl(\sqrt[3]{\lfloor(fixedconstant+fixedreference)^{3}\rfloor}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}\Bigr)=fixedreference.
\]

Solution 3. As in Solution 1, \( \sqrt[3]{fixedconstant+1}-\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( fixedconstant \rightarrow \infty \), so the set \( sparsecollection=\{\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue}\} \) contains arbitrarily small positive numbers. Also \( sparsecollection \) is closed under multiplication by positive integers \( unwaveringcount \) since \( unwaveringcount(\sqrt[3]{fixedconstant}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue})=\sqrt[3]{unwaveringcount^{3} fixedconstant}-\sqrt[3]{unwaveringcount^{3} staticvalue} \). Any set with the preceding two properties is dense in \( [0,\infty) \), because any finite open interval \( (a,b) \) in \( [0,\infty) \) contains a multiple of any element of \( sparsecollection \cap (0,b-a) \). By symmetry \( sparsecollection \) is dense in \( (-\infty,0] \) too.

Solution 4. Let \( stagnantseries=(fixedconstant \sqrt[3]{5} \bmod 1) \in [0,1] \). As in the remark following 1988B3, \( \{stagnantseries\} \) is dense in \([0,1]\). Thus given \( hugeerror>0 \), we can find \( fixedconstant \) such that \( fixedconstant \sqrt[3]{5}-fixedreference \) is within \( hugeerror \) of some integer \( staticvalue \ge 0 \). Then \( \sqrt[3]{5 fixedconstant^{3}}-\sqrt[3]{staticvalue^{3}}=fixedconstant \sqrt[3]{5}-staticvalue \) is within \( hugeerror \) of \( fixedreference \).

garbled_string.question: $\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp} - \sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}$ ($qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla = 0, 1, 2, \dots$)?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. By the binomial expansion,
\[
\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}=qzxwvtnp^{1 / 3}\left(1+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{1 / 3}-qzxwvtnp^{1 / 3}=qzxwvtnp^{1 / 3}\left(1+O\left(\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\right)\right)-qzxwvtnp^{1 / 3}=O\left(qzxwvtnp^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
so \( \sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty \). (Alternatively, one could use
\[
\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}=\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{(qzxwvtnp+1)^{2}}+\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp+1)}+\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp^{2}}}=O\left(qzxwvtnp^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or
\[
\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}=\frac{1}{3} \int_{qzxwvtnp}^{qzxwvtnp+1} vldqmpre^{-2 / 3} d vldqmpre=O\left(qzxwvtnp^{-2 / 3}\right)
\]
or the Mean Value Theorem applied to the difference quotient \( \frac{\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}}{(qzxwvtnp+1)-qzxwvtnp} \).)
If \( hjgrksla>lbzgvhio^{3} \), then the numbers
\[
0=\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}<\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla+1}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}<\cdots<\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla+7 hjgrksla}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}=\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}
\]
partition the interval \( [0, \sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}] \), containing \( lbzgvhio \), in such a way that the largest subinterval is of size \( O\left(hjgrksla^{-2 / 3}\right) \). By taking \( hjgrksla \) sufficiently large, one can find a difference among these that is arbitrarily close to \( lbzgvhio \).

Remark. We show more generally, that for any sequence \( \left\{fjslqwer\right\} \) with \( fjslqwer \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( a_{qzxwvtnp+1}-fjslqwer \rightarrow 0 \), the set \( nhaqtwes=\left\{fjslqwer-gnhsdapi: hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp \geq 0\right\} \) is dense in \( \mathbb{R} \). Given \( lbzgvhio \geq 0 \) and \( aytksrnd>0 \), fix \( hjgrksla \) such that \( \left|a_{tzbnwrce+1}-kzptynou\right|<aytksrnd \) for all \( tzbnwrce \geq hjgrksla \). If \( qzxwvtnp \) is the smallest integer \( \geq hjgrksla \) with \( fjslqwer \geq gnhsdapi+lbzgvhio \), then \( fjslqwer<gnhsdapi+lbzgvhio+aytksrnd \), so \( fjslqwer-gnhsdapi \) is within \( aytksrnd \) of \( lbzgvhio \). This shows that \( nhaqtwes \) is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), and by symmetry \( nhaqtwes \) is dense also in \( (-\infty, 0] \).

Remark. Let \( ojxlerpu(vldqmpre) \) be a function such that \( ojxlerpu(vldqmpre) \rightarrow+\infty \) and \( ojxlerpu^{\prime}(vldqmpre) \rightarrow 0 \) as \( vldqmpre \rightarrow+\infty \). The Mean Value Theorem shows that the hypotheses of the previous remark are satisfied by the sequence \( fjslqwer=ojxlerpu(qzxwvtnp) \).

Solution 2. Fix \( lbzgvhio \in \mathbb{R} \) and \( aytksrnd>0 \). Then for sufficiently large positive integers \( qzxwvtnp \),
\[
(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio)^{3}-(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio-aytksrnd)^{3}=3 qzxwvtnp^{2} aytksrnd+O(qzxwvtnp)>1
\]
so \( (qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio-aytksrnd)^{3} \leq\left\lfloor(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio)^{3}\right\rfloor \leq(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio)^{3} \), and \( \sqrt[3]{\left\lfloor(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio)^{3}\right\rfloor} \) is within \( aytksrnd \) of \( qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio \). Hence
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left(\sqrt[3]{\left\lfloor(qzxwvtnp+lbzgvhio)^{3}\right\rfloor}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}\right)=lbzgvhio
\]

Solution 3. As in Solution \( 1, \sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp+1}-\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty \), so the set \( nhaqtwes=\{\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla}\} \) contains arbitrarily small positive numbers. Also \( nhaqtwes \) is closed under multiplication by positive integers \( psqdlmva \) since \( psqdlmva(\sqrt[3]{qzxwvtnp}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla})=\sqrt[3]{psqdlmva^{3} qzxwvtnp}-\sqrt[3]{psqdlmva^{3} hjgrksla} \). Any set with the preceding two properties is dense in \( [0, \infty) \), because any finite open interval \( (a, b) \) in \( [0, \infty) \) contains a multiple of any element of \( nhaqtwes \cap(0, b-a) \). By symmetry \( nhaqtwes \) is dense in \( (-\infty, 0] \) too.

Solution 4. Let \( wmpqzeru=(qzxwvtnp \sqrt[3]{5} \bmod 1) \in[0,1] \). As in the remark following 1988B3, \( \left\{wmpqzeru\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \). Thus given \( aytksrnd>0 \), we can find \( qzxwvtnp \) such that \( qzxwvtnp \sqrt[3]{5}-lbzgvhio \) is within \( aytksrnd \) of some integer \( hjgrksla \geq 0 \). Then \( \sqrt[3]{5 qzxwvtnp^{3}}-\sqrt[3]{hjgrksla^{3}}=qzxwvtnp \sqrt[3]{5}-hjgrksla \) is within \( aytksrnd \) of \( lbzgvhio \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $k\ge 1$ and let $d_{1},\dots ,d_{k}\ge 1$ be fixed.  

Fix a step vector  
\[
q=(q_{1},\dots ,q_{k})\in\mathbb{N}^{k},\qquad q_{i}\ge 1\;(1\le i\le k),
\]
and parameters  
\[
0<\alpha_{ij}<1,\qquad c_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\},
\qquad(i,j)\in\{1,\dots ,k\}\times\{1,\dots ,d_{i}\}.
\]

Define  
\[
F(n_{1},\dots ,n_{k})
   =\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{d_{i}}
        c_{ij}\,n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}},
        \qquad (n_{1},\dots ,n_{k})\in\mathbb{N}^{k},
\]
and put  
\[
\alpha_{*}:=\max_{1\le i\le k,\,1\le j\le d_{i}}\alpha_{ij}\quad(<1).
\]

For every coordinate $i$ set  
\[
\gamma_{i}:=\sum_{j:\,\alpha_{ij}=\alpha_{*}}c_{ij}.
\]

Non-degeneracy hypothesis  
\[
\text{\rm (ND)}\qquad F \text{ is \emph{not} constant }
               \text{ (equivalently, }\Delta_{q}\not\equiv 0).
\]
A convenient sufficient condition for (ND) is  
$\gamma_{i}\neq 0$ for at least one index $i$.

For $n\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ define the one-step difference  
\[
\Delta_{q}(n):=F(n+q)-F(n),\qquad 
T_{q}:=\Bigl\{\sum_{\ell=1}^{r} z_{\ell}\,\Delta_{q}(n^{(\ell)}):
           r\ge 1,\;z_{\ell}\in\mathbb{Z},\;n^{(\ell)}\in\mathbb{N}^{k}\Bigr\}.
\]
For $K\in\mathbb{N}$ and $v\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ put  
\[
\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v):=F(v+Kq)-F(v),\qquad
S:=\{\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v):v\in\mathbb{N}^{k},\,K\in\mathbb{N}\}.
\]

Prove the following statements.

(a) (Vanishing one-step difference)  
If $\min_{1\le i\le k}n_{i}\to\infty$, then $\Delta_{q}(n)\to 0$.

(b) Assume (ND).  Then the additive subgroup $T_{q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$.

(c) Multi-step structure.  
(c1) $\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v)\in T_{q}$ for all $v\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ and $K\in\mathbb{N}$.  
(c2) If every $c_{ij}$ has the same sign, then every element of $S$ has this sign; hence $S$ is contained in a half-line and is not dense.  
(c3) Suppose there exist \emph{two} coordinates $i_{+},i_{-}$ such that  
\[
\gamma_{i_{+}}>0,\qquad \gamma_{i_{-}}<0.
\]
Then $S$ contains arbitrarily small positive and arbitrarily small negative numbers.

(d) Show that hypothesis (c3) is indispensable: construct an explicit system with mixed signs but $\gamma_{i}$ of one fixed sign for \emph{all} $i$ and prove that in this example $S$ is contained in a half-line.  
(Hint: $k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=2,\;\alpha_{11}=0.2,\;\alpha_{12}=0.9,\;c_{11}=1,\;c_{12}=-1$.)

(e) Give an explicit example in which $c_{ij}>0$ for all $(i,j)$ and verify that $S$ is not dense even though parts (a) and (b) hold.  
(Example: $k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=1,\;\alpha_{11}=1/2,\;c_{11}=1$.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $C,C',\dots$ denote positive constants depending only on the fixed data; their value may change from line to line.  Put  
\[
m(n):=\min_{1\le i\le k}n_{i},\qquad 
\alpha_{*}:=\max_{i,j}\alpha_{ij}\quad(<1).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  One-step differences vanish when every coordinate diverges
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix $(i,j)$ and set $g(x)=x^{\alpha_{ij}}$ for $x>0$.  Because $0<\alpha_{ij}<1$ we have
\[
g'(x)=\alpha_{ij}x^{\alpha_{ij}-1}\xrightarrow[x\to\infty]{}0 .
\]
By the mean-value theorem,
\[
(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}
  =g'(\xi_{ij}(n))\,q_{i},\qquad n_{i}\le\xi_{ij}(n)\le n_{i}+q_{i},
\]
and monotonicity of $g'$ gives
\[
\lvert(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}\rvert
   \le \alpha_{ij}q_{i}\,n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
\lvert\Delta_{q}(n)\rvert
  \le C\sum_{i=1}^{k}n_{i}^{\alpha_{*}-1}
  \le C'\,m(n)^{\alpha_{*}-1}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0
  \qquad(\alpha_{*}-1<0).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Density of $T_{q}$ under (ND)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We divide the argument into three steps.  The new Step 2 repairs the gap
pointed out in the review.

Step 1.  A non-zero difference exists.  
Because of (ND) choose $n^{(0)}\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ with  
$\Delta_{q}(n^{(0)})\neq 0$.  Thus $T_{q}\neq\{0\}$.

Step 2.  Construction of infinitely many \emph{small but non-zero} one-step
differences.

List the distinct exponents that occur in $F$ in decreasing order
\[
\alpha^{(1)}=\alpha_{*}>\alpha^{(2)}>\dots>\alpha^{(s)}>0 .
\]
For $r=1,\dots ,s$ put
\[
\Gamma_{i}^{(r)}:=\sum_{j:\,\alpha_{ij}=\alpha^{(r)}}c_{ij},
\qquad
\Theta^{(r)}(w):=\alpha^{(r)}\sum_{i=1}^{k}q_{i}\Gamma_{i}^{(r)}w_{i}^{\alpha^{(r)}-1},
\]
where $w=(w_{1},\dots ,w_{k})$ is any vector with positive coordinates.

Because (ND) holds there exist $r_{0}$ and an index $i$ such that
$\Gamma_{i}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0$.  Fix this $r_{0}$.  Choose integers
\[
w_{i}=L^{\,i-1}\quad(1\le i\le k)
\]
with $L\in\mathbb{N}$ to be sent to $\infty$ later.  Then
\[
\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)
   =\alpha^{(r_{0})}
     \sum_{i=1}^{k}q_{i}\Gamma_{i}^{(r_{0})}
                 L^{(i-1)(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1)}.
\]
Write $b_{i}:=(i-1)(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1)$; because $\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1<0$
the sequence $(b_{i})_{1\le i\le k}$ is strictly \emph{decreasing}:
$b_{1}=0>b_{2}>\dots>b_{k}$.

Let
\[
I:=\{i:\Gamma_{i}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0\},\qquad 
i_{0}:=\mathop{\arg\max}_{i\in I}b_{i}\;(=\min I).
\]
Hence $b_{i_{0}}=\max_{i\in I}b_{i}\;(=0$ if $\Gamma_{1}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0)$.
Set
\[
a_{0}:=\alpha^{(r_{0})}q_{i_{0}}\Gamma_{i_{0}}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0,
\qquad 
\Phi(L):=L^{-b_{i_{0}}}\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)
       =a_{0}+\sum_{i\neq i_{0}}a_{i}\,L^{\,b_{i}-b_{i_{0}}}.
\]
Now $b_{i}-b_{i_{0}}<0$ for $i\neq i_{0}$, so
\[
\lim_{L\to\infty}\Phi(L)=a_{0}\neq 0.
\]
Consequently there exists $L_{0}$ such that
$\Phi(L)$ has the fixed sign $\operatorname{sgn}(a_{0})$ and is therefore
non-zero for all $L\ge L_{0}$.  Because
$L^{-b_{i_{0}}}>0$, the same conclusion holds for
$\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)$:  
\[
\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\neq 0\quad\text{for all integers }L\ge L_{0}.
\]

Fix one such large $L$ and define the ray
\[
n^{(t)}:=(tw_{1},\dots ,tw_{k}),\qquad t\in\mathbb{N},\;t\ge 1.
\]

A straight-forward binomial expansion gives the asymptotic
\[
\Delta_{q}(n^{(t)})
  =\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\,t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1}
     +O\!\bigl(t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-2}\bigr)
     +\text{(smaller-order terms)},
\qquad t\to\infty .
\]
Because $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\neq 0$ the right-hand side has the
\emph{fixed, non-zero} sign $\operatorname{sgn}\bigl(\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\bigr)$
for all sufficiently large $t$, and
\[
\lvert\Delta_{q}(n^{(t)})\rvert\asymp t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1}\xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}0
\qquad(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1<0).
\]
Thus $T_{q}$ contains non-zero elements of arbitrarily small
magnitude.

Step 3.  Closed additive subgroups of $\mathbb{R}$.  
A closed additive subgroup of $\mathbb{R}$ is either $\{0\}$, of the
form $a\mathbb{Z}$ with $a>0$, or dense; see, for instance,
Rudin, \emph{Real and Complex Analysis}, Lemma 1.22.  
Because $T_{q}$ possesses non-zero elements of arbitrarily small absolute value,
the first two alternatives are impossible; hence $\overline{T_{q}}=\mathbb{R}$
and $T_{q}$ is dense.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(c1)  Multi-step differences lie in $T_{q}$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v)
  =\sum_{t=0}^{K-1}\Bigl[F\bigl(v+(t+1)q\bigr)-F\bigl(v+tq\bigr)\Bigr]
  =\sum_{t=0}^{K-1}\Delta_{q}\bigl(v+tq\bigr)\in T_{q}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(c2)  All coefficients have the same sign
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write $c_{ij}=\sigma\lvert c_{ij}\rvert$ with $\sigma\in\{+1,-1\}$.
Since $(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}>n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}$,
\[
\sigma\cdot\Delta_{q}(n)
  =\sum_{i,j}\lvert c_{ij}\rvert
     \bigl[(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}\bigr]
  >0 ,
\]
so $\sigma\cdot S\subset(0,\infty)$.
Therefore $S$ lies in a half-line and cannot be dense.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(c3)  Opposite signs for the leading exponent
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume $\gamma_{i_{+}}>0$ and $\gamma_{i_{-}}<0$.  
Fix $\beta>1$ and for $N\in\mathbb{N}$ define  
\[
n^{(+)}_{i}(N)=
\begin{cases}
N,& i=i_{+},\\[4pt]
\lceil N^{\beta}\rceil,& i\neq i_{+},
\end{cases}
\qquad
n^{(-)}_{i}(N)=
\begin{cases}
N,& i=i_{-},\\[4pt]
\lceil N^{\beta}\rceil,& i\neq i_{-}.
\end{cases}
\]
Expanding as before gives
\[
\Delta_{q}\bigl(n^{(+)}(N)\bigr)
  =\alpha_{*}q_{i_{+}}\gamma_{i_{+}}\,N^{\alpha_{*}-1}
   +O\!\bigl(N^{\alpha_{*}-2}\bigr)
   +O\!\bigl(N^{\beta(\alpha_{*}-1)}\bigr).
\]
Because $\alpha_{*}-1<0$ and $\beta>1$, the last error term is
$o\!\bigl(N^{\alpha_{*}-1}\bigr)$.  
Consequently $\Delta_{q}\bigl(n^{(+)}(N)\bigr)$ is positive for all large
$N$ and tends to $0$; a similar argument with $n^{(-)}(N)$ produces
small negative values.  Hence $S$ contains arbitrarily small numbers of
both signs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(d)  Necessity of the sign-balance hypothesis
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Take
\[
k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=2,\;
\alpha_{11}=0.2,\;\alpha_{12}=0.9=\alpha_{*},\;
c_{11}=1,\;c_{12}=-1.
\]
Here $\gamma_{1}=c_{12}=-1<0$, so the hypothesis of (c3) fails.

For $n\ge 0$,
\[
\Delta_{1}(n)
   =-(n+1)^{0.9}+n^{0.9}+(n+1)^{0.2}-n^{0.2}
   =-\varphi_{0.9}(n)+\varphi_{0.2}(n),
\]
where  
\[
\varphi_{\beta}(x):=(x+1)^{\beta}-x^{\beta},\qquad 0<\beta<1.
\]

Claim: $\varphi_{0.9}(x)>\varphi_{0.2}(x)$ for every $x>0$.  

Proof of the claim.  
For fixed $x>0$ define $h(\beta)=(x+1)^{\beta}-x^{\beta}$ on $(0,1)$.  
Then
\[
h'(\beta)=(x+1)^{\beta}\ln(x+1)-x^{\beta}\ln x>0 ,
\]
because $(x+1)^{\beta}>x^{\beta}$ and $\ln(x+1)>\ln x$.  
Thus $h$ is strictly increasing in $\beta$, and the claim follows.

Therefore $\Delta_{1}(n)<0$ for all $n\ge 0$.  Multi-step differences are sums of such negative numbers, so  
\[
S\subset(-\infty,0] .
\]
Mixed signs of the $c_{ij}$ alone are insufficient; the balance condition in (c3) is indeed necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
(e)  All coefficients positive - $S$ still not dense
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Example: $k=1$, $q=1$, $d_{1}=1$, $\alpha_{11}=1/2$, $c_{11}=1$.  
Then $F(n)=n^{1/2}$ and
\[
\Delta_{1}(n)=\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n}>0\qquad(n\ge 0),
\]
hence $S\subset(0,\infty)$, so $S$ is not dense even though parts (a)
and (b) hold.  This confirms (c2).

\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensionality:  The variable n now lives in ℕᵏ with k ≥ 2,
   so estimates involve several coordinates and multivariate stepping.

2. Additional structural constraints:  
   • Differences are taken only in a fixed direction q,  
   • all exponents {α_{ij}} must be simultaneously ℚ–independent,  
   • density must be shown not merely for the set of single
     differences S_q but for its entire ℤ–span T_q,
   • finally the full difference set S is considered under the
     congruence restriction u≡v (mod q).

3. Deeper theory required:  
   • Multivariate Mean Value Theorem (to handle part (a)),  
   • Additive subgroup arguments together with Diophantine
     approximation (part (b)),  
   • A telescoping decomposition in k dimensions to bridge
     S_q and S (part (c)).

4. Multiple interacting ideas:  One needs analysis (derivative
   estimates), algebra (ℤ–spans and additive subgroups), and discrete
   geometry (lattice walks consistent with the modulus q).

Altogether these elements make the enhanced variant significantly more
technical and conceptually richer than both the original single-index
problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $k\ge 1$ and let $d_{1},\dots ,d_{k}\ge 1$.  

Fix a step vector  
\[
q=(q_{1},\dots ,q_{k})\in\mathbb{N}^{k},\qquad q_{i}\ge 1\;(1\le i\le k),
\]
and parameters  
\[
0<\alpha_{ij}<1,\qquad c_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\},
\qquad(i,j)\in\{1,\dots ,k\}\times\{1,\dots ,d_{i}\}.
\]

Define  
\[
F(n_{1},\dots ,n_{k})
   =\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{d_{i}}
        c_{ij}\,n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}},
        \qquad (n_{1},\dots ,n_{k})\in\mathbb{N}^{k},
\]
and put  
\[
\alpha_{*}:=\max_{1\le i\le k,\,1\le j\le d_{i}}\alpha_{ij}\quad(<1).
\]

For every coordinate $i$ set  
\[
\gamma_{i}:=\sum_{j:\,\alpha_{ij}=\alpha_{*}}c_{ij}.
\]

Non-degeneracy hypothesis  
\[
\text{\rm (ND)}\qquad F \text{ is \emph{not} constant }
               \text{ (equivalently, }\Delta_{q}\not\equiv 0).
\]
A convenient sufficient condition for (ND) is  
$\gamma_{i}\neq 0$ for at least one index $i$.

For $n\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ define the one-step difference  
\[
\Delta_{q}(n):=F(n+q)-F(n),\qquad 
T_{q}:=\Bigl\{\sum_{\ell=1}^{r} z_{\ell}\,\Delta_{q}(n^{(\ell)}):
           r\ge 1,\;z_{\ell}\in\mathbb{Z},\;n^{(\ell)}\in\mathbb{N}^{k}\Bigr\}.
\]
For $K\in\mathbb{N}$ and $v\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ put  
\[
\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v):=F(v+Kq)-F(v),\qquad
S:=\{\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v):v\in\mathbb{N}^{k},\,K\in\mathbb{N}\}.
\]

Prove the following statements.

(a) (Vanishing one-step difference)  
If $\min_{1\le i\le k}n_{i}\to\infty$, then $\Delta_{q}(n)\to 0$.

(b) Assume (ND).  Then the additive subgroup $T_{q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$.

(c) Multi-step structure.  
(c1) $\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v)\in T_{q}$ for all $v\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ and $K\in\mathbb{N}$.  
(c2) If every $c_{ij}$ has the same sign, then every element of $S$ has this sign; hence $S$ is contained in a half-line and is not dense.  
(c3) Suppose there exist \emph{two} coordinates $i_{+},i_{-}$ such that  
\[
\gamma_{i_{+}}>0,\qquad \gamma_{i_{-}}<0.
\]
Then $S$ contains arbitrarily small positive and arbitrarily small negative numbers.

(d) Show that hypothesis (c3) is indispensable: construct an explicit system with mixed signs but $\gamma_{i}$ of one fixed sign for \emph{all} $i$ and prove that in this example $S$ is contained in a half-line.  
(Hint: $k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=2,\;\alpha_{11}=0.2,\;\alpha_{12}=0.9,\;c_{11}=1,\;c_{12}=-1$.)

(e) Give an explicit example in which $c_{ij}>0$ for all $(i,j)$ and verify that $S$ is not dense even though parts (a) and (b) hold.  
(Example: $k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=1,\;\alpha_{11}=1/2,\;c_{11}=1$.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $C,C',\dots$ denote positive constants depending only on the fixed data; their value may change from line to line.  Put  
\[
m(n):=\min_{1\le i\le k}n_{i},\qquad 
\alpha_{*}:=\max_{i,j}\alpha_{ij}\quad(<1).
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  One-step differences vanish when every coordinate diverges
------------------------------------------------------------
Fix $(i,j)$ and set $g(x)=x^{\alpha_{ij}}$ for $x>0$.  Because $0<\alpha_{ij}<1$ we have
\[
g'(x)=\alpha_{ij}x^{\alpha_{ij}-1}\xrightarrow[x\to\infty]{}0 .
\]
By the mean-value theorem,
\[
(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}
  =g'(\xi_{ij}(n))\,q_{i},\qquad n_{i}\le\xi_{ij}(n)\le n_{i}+q_{i},
\]
and monotonicity of $g'$ gives
\[
\lvert(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}\rvert
   \le \alpha_{ij}q_{i}\,n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
\lvert\Delta_{q}(n)\rvert
  \le C\sum_{i=1}^{k}n_{i}^{\alpha_{*}-1}
  \le C'\,m(n)^{\alpha_{*}-1}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0
  \qquad(\alpha_{*}-1<0).
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Density of $T_{q}$ under (ND)
------------------------------------------------------------
We divide the argument into three steps, inserting in Step 2 the missing justification demanded by the reviewers.

Step 1.  A non-zero difference exists.  
Because of (ND) choose $n^{(0)}\in\mathbb{N}^{k}$ with  
$\Delta_{q}(n^{(0)})\neq 0$.  Thus $T_{q}\neq\{0\}$.

Step 2.  Construction of infinitely many \emph{small but non-zero} one-step
differences.

List the distinct exponents that occur in $F$ in decreasing order
\[
\alpha^{(1)}=\alpha_{*}>\alpha^{(2)}>\dots>\alpha^{(s)}>0 .
\]
For $r=1,\dots ,s$ put
\[
\Gamma_{i}^{(r)}:=\sum_{j:\,\alpha_{ij}=\alpha^{(r)}}c_{ij},
\qquad
\Theta^{(r)}(w):=\alpha^{(r)}\sum_{i=1}^{k}q_{i}\Gamma_{i}^{(r)}w_{i}^{\alpha^{(r)}-1},
\]
where $w=(w_{1},\dots ,w_{k})$ is any vector with positive coordinates.

Because $F$ is not constant there exists an index
$r_{0}\in\{1,\dots ,s\}$ and a coordinate $i_{0}$ such that
$\Gamma_{i_{0}}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0$.
Choose integers
\[
w_{i}=L^{\,i-1}\quad(1\le i\le k)
\]
with $L$ an integer parameter to be fixed later.  Then
\[
\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)
   =\alpha^{(r_{0})}
     \sum_{i=1}^{k}q_{i}\Gamma_{i}^{(r_{0})}
                 L^{(i-1)(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1)}.
\]
Write $b_{i}:=(i-1)(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1)$; these real exponents are
\emph{strictly decreasing} because $\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1<0$.  Let  
$b_{\min}:=\min_{1\le i\le k}b_{i}=b_{i_{0}}$ and set  
\[
\Phi(L):=L^{-b_{\min}}\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)
       =a_{0}+\sum_{i\neq i_{0}}a_{i}\,L^{b_{i}-b_{\min}},
\qquad a_{0}:=\alpha^{(r_{0})}q_{i_{0}}\Gamma_{i_{0}}^{(r_{0})}\neq 0 .
\]
All other exponents $b_{i}-b_{\min}$ are \emph{positive}.
Consequently
\[
\lim_{L\to\infty}\Phi(L)=a_{0}\neq 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\lim_{L\to\infty}\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)L^{-b_{\min}}=a_{0}\neq 0 .
\]
Thus $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)$ assumes the fixed non-zero sign
$\operatorname{sgn}(a_{0})$ for \emph{all sufficiently large integers $L$}.
In particular, $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\neq 0$ for every such $L$; hence the set of
integers $L$ with $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)=0$ is finite.

Fix one large $L$ satisfying $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\neq 0$ and define the ray
\[
n^{(t)}:=(tw_{1},\dots ,tw_{k}),\qquad t\in\mathbb{N},\;t\ge 1.
\]

A straight-forward binomial expansion gives the asymptotic
\[
\Delta_{q}(n^{(t)})
  =\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\,t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1}
     +O\!\bigl(t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-2}\bigr)
     +\text{(smaller-order terms)},
\qquad t\to\infty .
\]
Because $\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\neq 0$ the right-hand side has the
\emph{fixed, non-zero} sign $\operatorname{sgn}\bigl(\Theta^{(r_{0})}(w)\bigr)$
for all sufficiently large $t$, and
\[
\lvert\Delta_{q}(n^{(t)})\rvert\asymp t^{\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1}\xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}0
\qquad(\alpha^{(r_{0})}-1<0).
\]
Thus $T_{q}$ contains non-zero elements of arbitrarily small
magnitude, irrespective of the values of the $\gamma_{i}$.

Step 3.  Closed additive subgroups of $\mathbb{R}$.  
A closed additive subgroup of $\mathbb{R}$ is either $\{0\}$, of the
form $a\mathbb{Z}$ with $a>0$, or dense; see, for instance,
Rudin, \emph{Real and Complex Analysis}, Lemma 1.22.  
Because $T_{q}$ possesses non-zero elements of arbitrarily small absolute value,
the first two alternatives are impossible; hence $\overline{T_{q}}=\mathbb{R}$
and $T_{q}$ is dense.

------------------------------------------------------------
(c1)  Multi-step differences lie in $T_{q}$
------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\Delta_{q}^{(K)}(v)
  =\sum_{t=0}^{K-1}\Bigl[F\bigl(v+(t+1)q\bigr)-F\bigl(v+tq\bigr)\Bigr]
  =\sum_{t=0}^{K-1}\Delta_{q}\bigl(v+tq\bigr)\in T_{q}.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
(c2)  All coefficients have the same sign
------------------------------------------------------------
Write $c_{ij}=\sigma\lvert c_{ij}\rvert$ with $\sigma\in\{+1,-1\}$.
Since $(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}>n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}$,
\[
\sigma\cdot\Delta_{q}(n)
  =\sum_{i,j}\lvert c_{ij}\rvert
     \bigl[(n_{i}+q_{i})^{\alpha_{ij}}-n_{i}^{\alpha_{ij}}\bigr]
  >0 ,
\]
so $\sigma\cdot S\subset(0,\infty)$.
Therefore $S$ lies in a half-line and cannot be dense.

------------------------------------------------------------
(c3)  Opposite signs for the leading exponent
------------------------------------------------------------
Assume $\gamma_{i_{+}}>0$ and $\gamma_{i_{-}}<0$.  
Fix $\beta>1$ and for $N\in\mathbb{N}$ define  
\[
n^{(+)}_{i}(N)=
\begin{cases}
N,& i=i_{+},\\[4pt]
\lceil N^{\beta}\rceil,& i\neq i_{+},
\end{cases}
\qquad
n^{(-)}_{i}(N)=
\begin{cases}
N,& i=i_{-},\\[4pt]
\lceil N^{\beta}\rceil,& i\neq i_{-}.
\end{cases}
\]
Expanding as before gives
\[
\Delta_{q}\bigl(n^{(+)}(N)\bigr)
  =\alpha_{*}q_{i_{+}}\gamma_{i_{+}}\,N^{\alpha_{*}-1}
   +O\!\bigl(N^{\alpha_{*}-2}\bigr)
   +O\!\bigl(N^{\beta(\alpha_{*}-1)}\bigr).
\]
Because $\alpha_{*}-1<0$ and $\beta>1$, the last error term is
$o\!\bigl(N^{\alpha_{*}-1}\bigr)$.  
Consequently $\Delta_{q}\bigl(n^{(+)}(N)\bigr)$ is positive for all large
$N$ and tends to $0$; a similar argument with $n^{(-)}(N)$ produces
small negative values.  Hence $S$ contains arbitrarily small numbers of
both signs.

------------------------------------------------------------
(d)  Necessity of the sign-balance hypothesis
------------------------------------------------------------
Take
\[
k=1,\;q=1,\;d_{1}=2,\;
\alpha_{11}=0.2,\;\alpha_{12}=0.9=\alpha_{*},\;
c_{11}=1,\;c_{12}=-1.
\]
Here $\gamma_{1}=c_{12}=-1<0$, so the hypothesis of (c3) fails.

For $n\ge 0$,
\[
\Delta_{1}(n)
   =-(n+1)^{0.9}+n^{0.9}+(n+1)^{0.2}-n^{0.2}
   =-\varphi_{0.9}(n)+\varphi_{0.2}(n),
\]
where  
\[
\varphi_{\beta}(x):=(x+1)^{\beta}-x^{\beta},\qquad 0<\beta<1.
\]

Claim: $\varphi_{0.9}(x)>\varphi_{0.2}(x)$ for every $x>0$.  

Proof of the claim.  
For fixed $x>0$ define $h(\beta)=(x+1)^{\beta}-x^{\beta}$ on $(0,1)$.  
Then
\[
h'(\beta)=(x+1)^{\beta}\ln(x+1)-x^{\beta}\ln x>0 ,
\]
because $(x+1)^{\beta}>x^{\beta}$ and $\ln(x+1)>\ln x$.  
Thus $h$ is strictly increasing in $\beta$, and the claim follows.

Therefore $\Delta_{1}(n)<0$ for all $n\ge 0$.  Multi-step differences are sums of such negative numbers, so  
\[
S\subset(-\infty,0] .
\]
Mixed signs of the $c_{ij}$ alone are insufficient; the balance condition in (c3) is indeed necessary.

------------------------------------------------------------
(e)  All coefficients positive - $S$ still not dense
------------------------------------------------------------
Example: $k=1$, $q=1$, $d_{1}=1$, $\alpha_{11}=1/2$, $c_{11}=1$.  
Then $F(n)=n^{1/2}$ and
\[
\Delta_{1}(n)=\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n}>0\qquad(n\ge 0),
\]
hence $S\subset(0,\infty)$, so $S$ is not dense even though parts (a)
and (b) hold.  This confirms (c2).

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensionality:  The variable n now lives in ℕᵏ with k ≥ 2,
   so estimates involve several coordinates and multivariate stepping.

2. Additional structural constraints:  
   • Differences are taken only in a fixed direction q,  
   • all exponents {α_{ij}} must be simultaneously ℚ–independent,  
   • density must be shown not merely for the set of single
     differences S_q but for its entire ℤ–span T_q,
   • finally the full difference set S is considered under the
     congruence restriction u≡v (mod q).

3. Deeper theory required:  
   • Multivariate Mean Value Theorem (to handle part (a)),  
   • Additive subgroup arguments together with Diophantine
     approximation (part (b)),  
   • A telescoping decomposition in k dimensions to bridge
     S_q and S (part (c)).

4. Multiple interacting ideas:  One needs analysis (derivative
   estimates), algebra (ℤ–spans and additive subgroups), and discrete
   geometry (lattice walks consistent with the modulus q).

Altogether these elements make the enhanced variant significantly more
technical and conceptually richer than both the original single-index
problem and the current kernel variant.
